content.text: Generics and Protocols are two advanced type system features that make Python's type hints expressive enough to describe patterns that appear constantly in real libraries: reusable containers that work safely with any element type, and interface contracts that classes can satisfy without explicit inheritance.
Generics allow you to parameterise a class or function with one or more type variables, writing the logic once and instantiating it safely for any type. A

are the same code, but the checker treats them as distinct types—operations valid on one are invalid on the other. Protocols define structural interfaces: any class that implements the required methods satisfies the Protocol, whether or not it explicitly inherits from it. This is Python's take on structural typing, sometimes called duck typing with a static guarantee.
Without generics, reusable containers must fall back to
for their element types, which defeats the purpose of type annotations. A
—the caller loses all type information at that point. Generics solve this precisely: the type flows through the container untouched, so
Protocols solve a different problem: in Python, you frequently write functions that accept 'anything with a
'. Without Protocols, you have two bad options: use

(no type safety) or use ABC inheritance (forces all classes to explicitly inherit, even third-party ones you can't modify). Protocol gives you a third option: state the required interface, and the checker verifies any value passed to the function satisfies it—regardless of its inheritance chain.
Generics enable type-safe reuse: one implementation works for all types without sacrificing type information.
The checker infers the type parameter from context, so
is often inferred without explicit annotation.
Protocols express 'duck typing' contracts that are checked at development time rather than only at runtime.
Protocol lets you type-check against third-party classes without modifying them—structural rather than nominal.
) restrict generics to specific types, preventing misuse.
) constrain T to be a subtype of a base class, enabling calling base class methods on T.
Runtime-checkable Protocols enable isinstance() checks at runtime, not just statically.
overload, Literal, Final, and ClassVar extend the annotation system for advanced type safety needs.
Generic class definitions add visual noise (
TypeVar scoping rules have subtle edge cases—especially when the same TypeVar appears in multiple function parameters.
Protocols require care when a class satisfies a Protocol only partially—the checker verifies all required methods are present.
runtime_checkable Protocols only check method presence at runtime, not argument types—do not rely on them for deep validation.
Complex generic hierarchies (e.g., covariant and contravariant TypeVars) require significant type theory knowledge to design correctly.
is a placeholder for a concrete type that is resolved at each use site. When you write
appears in methods, the checker substitutes the concrete type bound at that use site (
). The substitution is purely a type-checking concept—the runtime sees a single class with no type information.

TypeVars can be unconstrained (any type), constrained (only specific types), or bounded (must be a subtype of some class). Constraints are checked at the call site—passing a type that does not match the constraint is a type error. Bounds enable you to call methods of the bound class on a generic value, because the checker knows
is guaranteed to be at least as capable as the bound.

class lists method signatures and attribute annotations. The checker considers any class that provides those signatures as satisfying the Protocol, regardless of inheritance. This is called structural subtyping (or structural typing). The nominal typing model (used by abstract base classes) requires explicit inheritance; structural typing does not.
At runtime, a plain Protocol is erased—isinstance() does not work on it by default. Adding
to a Protocol enables isinstance() checks, but the check only verifies the presence of the required methods (by name), not their signatures or return types.
Architecture: Generics and Protocols in the Type System

The diagram below shows how Generic[T] and Protocol work side-by-side. On the left, a single generic class is instantiated to multiple type-safe variants. On the right, multiple unrelated classes satisfy a Protocol without any inheritance relationship.

attrs.alt: Generics with TypeVar: Stack[int] and Stack[str] are type-safe instantiations of one generic class. Protocols: Circle, Rectangle, and SVGPath all satisfy Drawable through structural subtyping, without inheriting from it.

content.text: When to Use Generics vs Protocols
Use Generic[T] when you are writing a container or utility class that should work uniformly for any element type (Stack, Queue, Result, Repository).
Use Protocol when you want to express an interface that multiple unrelated classes should satisfy—particularly when those classes come from different codebases or libraries.
Use Protocol instead of ABC when you cannot or do not want to require explicit inheritance from an abstract base class.
Use constrained TypeVars when a function is valid for only a specific set of types (e.g., numeric operations requiring
Use bounded TypeVars when a function must call methods on T that are defined in some base class.
Use Literal when a parameter must be one of a fixed set of string or integer constants.
Use Final when a module-level constant or class attribute should never be reassigned.
Define a TypeVar at module level, then use it in your class body by inheriting from
. Annotate every method parameter and return that involves the type parameter with
. The checker will substitute the concrete type at each use site.

Define a Protocol class listing the required method signatures. Write functions that accept the Protocol type. Any class implementing those methods—whether from your own code, the standard library, or a third-party package—satisfies the Protocol automatically.
Beyond the core Generic[T] and Protocol patterns, the typing module provides several other features that extend type safety. The following example demonstrates
—all of which appear regularly in production Python code.

from __future__ import annotations
from typing import (
    Protocol,
    runtime_checkable,
    overload,
    Literal,
    Final,
    ClassVar,
    TypeVar,
    Generic,
)


# --- runtime_checkable: isinstance() works on Protocol ---
@runtime_checkable
class Sized(Protocol):
    def __len__(self) -> int: ...


print(isinstance([1, 2, 3], Sized))   # True
print(isinstance('hello', Sized))      # True
print(isinstance(42, Sized))           # False


# --- overload: multiple type signatures for one function ---
from typing import overload as _overload


@overload
def process(value: int) -> int: ...
@overload
def process(value: str) -> str: ...
def process(value: int | str) -> int | str:
    if isinstance(value, int):
        return value * 2
    return value.upper()


x: int = process(5)        # x is int
y: str = process('hello')  # y is str


# --- Literal: restrict to specific values ---
Mode = Literal['read', 'write', 'append']


def open_file(path: str, mode: Mode) -> None:
    print(f'Opening {path} in {mode} mode')


open_file('data.txt', 'read')    # OK
# open_file('data.txt', 'exec')  # error: not a valid Mode


# --- Final: variable that cannot be reassigned ---
MAX_RETRIES: Final = 3
API_VERSION: Final[str] = 'v2'

# MAX_RETRIES = 4   # mypy/pyright error: cannot assign to Final


# --- ClassVar: class-level attribute (not instance) ---
class Counter:
    count: ClassVar[int] = 0

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        Counter.count += 1
        self.id = Counter.count

    @classmethod
    def reset(cls) -> None:
        cls.count = 0


a = Counter()
b = Counter()
print(Counter.count)  # 2
# a.count = 5  # mypy error: cannot assign to ClassVar via instance
The Repository pattern is a common abstraction for data access: a repository object wraps a data source and exposes typed CRUD operations. Without generics, a repository for users returns
from every read method, and the caller loses type information immediately. With a generic repository,
—the caller gets full type information and autocompletion.
The Protocol pattern also appears in plug-in systems: a
. Any class implementing these methods—whether it wraps S3, a local filesystem, or a Redis cache—can be passed to functions that accept

. New backends are added without modifying the Protocol or any existing code. This combination of generics and protocols enables clean layered architectures where high-level code is decoupled from implementation details.
Using the same TypeVar in a way that does not constrain related parameters—e.g.,
def f(a: T, b: T) -> T
correctly constrains a, b, and the return to the same type, but
def f(a: T, b: T) -> None
with unrelated bodies may not behave as expected.
Forgetting that Protocols are not checked at runtime by default—do not write
Using runtime_checkable and assuming isinstance() verifies method signatures—it only checks presence by name.
—this is rarely correct; Protocol methods define the interface, not construction.
Nesting TypeVars inside function bodies—TypeVars must be declared at module or class level.
from the typing module in Python 3.9+ instead of
) and contravariance—start with invariant (default) until you understand the semantics.
Declare TypeVars at module level with a single-letter or descriptive name (
Keep Protocol classes focused—one Protocol per distinct interface rather than one large Protocol covering many concerns.
only when you actually need isinstance() checks; otherwise omit it.
Document TypeVar constraints clearly—a comment or docstring explaining what T represents saves future readers time.
Prefer Protocol over ABC for library code that must interoperate with types you do not control.
Test generic classes with multiple type instantiations (
) to ensure the generic code is actually correct for multiple types.
types for flag parameters and mode strings rather than bare
—it documents the valid values and lets the checker catch typos.
for module-level constants that should never change; the checker prevents accidental reassignment.
Like all type annotations, TypeVar, Generic, and Protocol are purely static constructs—they add no runtime overhead to method calls or attribute accesses. A
. The type parameters exist only in the checker's analysis; the runtime has no concept of them.

runtime_checkable Protocols do add a small overhead to isinstance() calls because Python must inspect the object's type for method presence. This is comparable to a regular isinstance() call and is not a concern in normal code paths. Avoid runtime_checkable in tight inner loops if profiling shows it matters.

For large codebases with many generic classes and Protocol hierarchies, static checker performance can increase. mypy's incremental mode and pyright's language server mode handle this well—only changed files and their dependants are re-checked. On a codebase with hundreds of generic classes, re-check time in the editor is still typically under a second.
mypy fully supports TypeVar, Generic, Protocol, and all advanced typing constructs from the typing module.
pyright provides fast Generic and Protocol checking and is the checker behind Pylance's in-editor type errors.
Pydantic v2 uses Generic[T] extensively—you can define
FastAPI leverages Generic response models for typed endpoint return values.
attrs supports Generic[T] annotations on class attributes, similar to dataclasses.
as a shorter syntax for Generic classes, avoiding the explicit TypeVar declaration.
typing_extensions backports newer typing features (ParamSpec, TypeAlias, Self, Unpack) to older Python versions.
beartype enforces Generic type parameters at runtime using its
The table below compares Generics and Protocols to the alternatives for the same use cases.
Generic classes improve reliability by preserving type information through container boundaries. Without generics, retrieving an item from a container returns

, which silences the checker for all subsequent operations on that value—a common source of bugs that are hard to trace. Generics ensure that type errors at container boundaries are caught at development time.

Protocols improve reliability in plugin-based or extensible systems. Instead of relying on runtime duck-typing errors ('AttributeError: has no attribute draw') to detect interface mismatches, Protocol lets the checker flag violations before deployment. In agentic AI systems, defining a Protocol for tool functions (
) ensures that any class registered as a tool is structurally valid before the agent tries to call it.
For generics, think of TypeVar as a blank type slot. When you write
for that instantiation. At runtime, the slot doesn't exist—but in the checker's model,

For protocols, think of them as shape definitions: 'I need something shaped like this.' Any object that fits the shape satisfies the Protocol. The classical OOP approach (ABC) says 'I need something that explicitly is-a AbstractBase.' Protocols say 'I need something that can-do these things.' The difference matters when working with code you cannot modify—a third-party class cannot inherit from your ABC, but it can satisfy your Protocol if it happens to implement the same interface.
Together, generics and protocols give Python's type system the expressive power needed to type real-world library code:
generics for reusable containers and utilities, protocols for structural interfaces that decouple components without forcing inheritance
parameterises a class, making the type flow through safely.
Protocol defines a structural interface—any class implementing the required methods satisfies it, regardless of inheritance.
) to restrict generics to specific types.
) when you need to call base class methods on the generic type.
to a Protocol only when you need isinstance() checks; note it only checks method names, not signatures.
overload, Literal, Final, and ClassVar extend type safety beyond basic annotations—use them for cleaner, more expressive APIs.
Generic and Protocol add zero runtime overhead—type parameters are a static analysis construct only.
In plugin and agent architectures, define the tool or backend as a Protocol to decouple interface from implementation.
